items.q: What was the Augustine vs Pelagius dispute about original sin?
items.correct: Augustine argued humans inherit a corrupted nature from Adam (original sin) and cannot achieve salvation without grace; Pelagius argued humans retain free will to choose good without divine intervention and that sin is imitated, not inherited.
items.misconception: Original sin means Christians believe babies are evil.
items.random_wrong: Pelagius and Augustine agreed humans are born innocent.
items.why: Original sin is about inherited nature/condition, not personal guilt of infants. The Pelagian controversy shaped Western Christianity — Augustine won, but the question of how far human nature is corrupted remains theologically contested.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: Did Augustine teach that sex within marriage is sinful in itself?
items.correct: No — he held marriage and procreation are goods; what is disordered after the Fall is concupiscence, the lust that accompanies the act, not the marital act itself.
items.misconception: Augustine taught that all sex, even within marriage, is inherently sinful and to be avoided.
items.random_wrong: Augustine taught that the Fall had no effect on human desire.
items.why: In On the Good of Marriage Augustine defends marriage against its despisers. His pessimism targets disordered desire, not the institution — a distinction exam answers regularly blur.

items.q: Did Pelagius deny that humans need God's grace in any sense?
items.correct: Not entirely — Pelagius affirmed grace as free will, the law, and forgiveness, but denied inherited original sin and held humans can choose the good; Augustine insisted on grace as inner transformation we cannot do without.
items.misconception: Pelagius taught that God gives no help of any kind and humans save themselves unaided.
items.random_wrong: Pelagius agreed with Augustine that humans inherit Adam's guilt.
items.why: The controversy is subtler than 'grace versus no grace': it concerns what grace is and whether the will is fatally wounded by the Fall. The crude version makes Augustine's victory look trivial.

items.q: Augustine's Latin phrase for fallen humanity's inability not to sin is:
items.why: Correct answer: non posse non peccare. “posse non peccare” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The famous opening prayer of the Confessions ends 'our heart is ___ until it rests in thee'.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: restless. “weary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately when did Augustine complete the Confessions?
items.why: Exact answer: 397. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
